title: Stephen Colbert's Legacy of Vulnerability
source: The Guardian - Time International Edition - June 08 2026
content:
Stephen Colbert achieved something extraordinary during his tenure as the host of The Late Show. Since taking over from original host David Letterman in 2015 until his final episode in May 2026, he made it entirely normal to talk about the hardest parts of being alive on television. Colbert was not just funny; he had a unique way of revealing the full scope of a guest's humanity. He created profound moments where difficult human experiences were not packaged into something easier to sit with or made tidy. Instead, these moments simply stayed there, unresolved, in front of millions of viewers.

A prime example was his 2021 interview with actor Andrew Garfield, who spoke openly about grieving his mother. Similarly, Colbert had many candid conversations with Anderson Cooper, reflecting on the deaths of Cooper's parents with a clarity that never rushed toward artificial closure. Colbert himself often returned to the tragic loss of his father and brothers in a plane crash when he was a child. These raw moments never pivoted neatly back to promotion or punch lines, but simply existed, often sandwiched between humorous and absurd segments. Ultimately, Colbert demonstrated that television can hold difficult human experiences without shaping them into something easily digestible.
